## Description

Allow negotiation of all use_srtp profile values currently listed by IANA, especially the AEAD modes from RFC7714(which all browsers already support for WebRTC)

This is a recreation of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10372 updated against the latest 4.x release codebase.

This does not touch or change any real cryptography logic. This is just about extending the list of permitted SRTP profile values to be negotiated, corresponding debug enum<=>string translation and tests. Without this change, Mbed TLS fails to negotiate any of these newer and more secure modes as part of a DTLS handshake.

Someone with reviewer privileges probably needs to apply corresponding labels to this pull request.

items.body:
## Description

This is a reference pr that can be used to replicate a baseline test for the `mbedtls_test_move_handshake_to_state` and `mbedtls_ssl_tls13_fetch_handshake_msg`

The states of the machines in TLS1.3 path are 

	
### Client path

~~~~~~
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HELLO_REQUEST (start)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_ENCRYPTED_EXTENSIONS (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CERTIFICATE_REQUEST (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_FINISHED (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_FINISHED (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_FLUSH_BUFFERS
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_WRAPUP
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_OVER
~~~~~~

### Server path

~~~~~~
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HELLO_REQUEST (start)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_ENCRYPTED_EXTENSIONS (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CERTIFICATE_REQUEST (optional, send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_FINISHED (send)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY (optional, recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_FINISHED (recv)
- MBEDTLS_SSL_FLUSH_BUFFERS
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_WRAPUP
- MBEDTLS_SSL_HANDSHAKE_OVER
~~~~~~

And the argumentation of the function can be found here -> [link](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/blob/development/tests/include/test/ssl_helpers.h#L528-L547)

The aim is to retrieve other contents of the handshake like CLIENT_HELLO/SERVER_HELLO

For example lets assume that we need to extract `MBEDTLS_SSL_HS_CLIENT_HELLO`

All the following combinations will fail with `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_WANT_READ`

~~~~~~
+--------------------------------------------+------------------------------+------------------------------+
| mbedtls_test_move_handshake_to_state ->    | Client                       | Server                       |
| mbedtls_ssl_tls13_fetch_handshake_msg      |                              |                              |
+--------------------------------------------+------------------------------+------------------------------+
| Endpoint                                   | Client state                 | Server state                 |
+--------------------------------------------+------------------------------+------------------------------+
| client_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     |
| client_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     |
| client_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO     |
| client_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO     |
+--------------------------------------------+------------------------------+------------------------------+
| server_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     |
| server_ep                                  | MBEDTLS_SSL_SERVER_HELLO     | MBEDTLS_SSL_CLIENT_HELLO     |

## Description

Replace usage of `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` guards in library code in favor of `PSA_WANT`.

The problem being resolved with this PR is that `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` is still used to guard TLS/X509 code. This is fine as long as builtin drivers are compiled because PSA_WANT are "[translated](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/blob/088771225c20d21306b2a94170dd7edf610d8589/include/tf-psa-crypto/build_info.h#L154)" to legacy symbols. However in builds where `MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C` is not enabled (as for example in TF-M) this creates a big problem:
- user is not allowed to set `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` in the configuration header file
- there is no auto-translation

Resolves #10618.
Resolves #10619.

In the TLS-Exporter for TLS 1.3 we mistakenly call PSA_HASH_LENGTH() on an mbedtls_md_type_t when it should be called on a psa_algorithm_t.

Fortunately, these two types have almost the same values, since we have previously aligned them to make conversion more efficient. As a result, PSA_HASH_LENGTH() produces exactly the same value when called on an mbedtls_md_type_t as with the equivalent psa_algorithm_t.

Thanks to this happy coincidence, fix a largely cosmetic issue (rather than a major functional bug).

Resolve https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/675

* Move feature requirement macros that need to be defined before including any system header to a new dedicated header `library/mbedtls_platform_requirements.h`.
* Redo feature requirement macros from scratch, so that they're simpler and justified. This could cause “exotic” platforms to break.

Done in previous pull requests:

* Simplify `timing.c` (in a way that's technically an API break, but come on, it's `timing.c` which is not used by any library module, only by test/sample programs). This removes the use of `gettimeofday()` (we already use `clock_gettime()` for `mbedtls_ms_time()`). https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10615
* Always use C99-compliant printf on MingW, removing the need for `MBEDTLS_PRINTF_SIZET` in `debug.h`. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10625

This is the last of a long chain of PR. Merge order:

1. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10615
2. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls-framework/pull/279 and https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10607
3. https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10625

I have tested:

* MinGW-w64 5 + wine on Ubuntu 18.04 x86_64 and x86_32

## Description

This is the 3.6 backport of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/687

Beyond contextual differences, some feature macros and error codes have been changed to adapt to 3.6. The commit structure of the original PR has been preserved, except the commit "Deprecate old macro and move to compat header" has not been preserved, and there are additional 3.6-specific commits at the end.

Indeed, just backporting #687 doest not fully resolve https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/476 for 3.6 as it has a few buffers that development doesn't have:
```
% gcc -Iinclude -Icore -I drivers/builtin/include -Os -fstack-usage -c library/pk*.c && rm pkcs*.su
% awk '{print $2" "$1}' pk*.su | egrep '^([0-9]{4}|[5-9][0-9]{2})' | sort -rn 
2464 library/pk.c:712:12:import_pair_into_psa.constprop
1168 library/pk_wrap.c:1335:12:rsa_alt_check_pair
1120 library/pk.c:806:12:import_public_into_psa.constprop
```
After the next three commits though, we're down to:
```
624 library/pk.c:792:12:import_public_into_psa.constprop
```
which I think is acceptable: the 2kB+ stack buffers are gone, as well as the 1kB buffers, and the only remaining one is 528 bytes - using the same [target](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/issues/476#issuecomment-3852996548) as for development.

This missed a few buffers used only when `MBEDTLS_USE_PSA_CRYPTO` is enabled, which were addressed in later commits. As of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10609/commits/c763c2e83717d276108c9c28c5c43d9d69e7ce7d in the default config + `USE_PSA` we're down to:
```
720 library/pk.c:1198:5:mbedtls_pk_verify_ext
704 library/pk_wrap.c:366:12:rsa_encrypt_wrap
672 library/pk_wrap.c:65:12:rsa_verify_wrap
640 library/pk.c:792:12:import_public_into_psa.constprop
```
which again sounds acceptable.

Note: the commit "PK: use smarter size for stack buffer" and "PK: use EC macro for EC key size" can be seen as a backport of the relevant bits of https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/679 - the commit "PK: use PSA size macros when exporting to PSA" is somewhat similar.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided (externally reported)
- [x] **crypto PR** provided  https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/687

items.body:
## Description

Parse and store all certificate policy OIDs instead of rejecting non-anyPolicy OIDs with MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE. Per RFC 5280 section 4.2.1.4, certificate policy enforcement is optional and should be left to the application. This aligns Mbed TLS behavior with OpenSSL, GnuTLS, and wolfSSL, which all accept certificates with custom/enterprise policy OIDs. Unknown policy OIDs are now rendered as numeric dotted strings (e.g. 1.2.3.4) in certificate info output.
                                                                                                                                                                       
Fixes #8123. Related to #8935, #4062, #7182.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            

## PR checklist

  - changelog provided
  - development PR provided # | not required because: this is the development PR
  - TF-PSA-Crypto PR not required because: no changes to TF-PSA-Crypto
  - framework PR not required
  - 3.6 PR not required because: should be discussed with maintainers for backport (v3.6.5 backport available here as reference https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/mbedtls/pull/80)
  - tests provided

### Notes

---                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
Why accepting all certificate policy OIDs is the correct fix

The problem in practice

When a TLS peer sends a certificate with a non-anyPolicy OID — such as the GSMA eSIM policy 2.23.146.1.2.1.0 — Mbed TLS rejects the entire certificate with MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE (0x2080). This happens during the TLS handshake itself, at a point where no application code has the opportunity to intervene.

The existing workaround requires three separate patches across two different codebases:

1. A custom callback function (gsma_x509_ext_cb) to accept the policy extension
2. A patch in Zephyr's sockets_tls.c to use mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_der_with_ext_cb() when loading CA certificates
3. A patch inside Mbed TLS itself in ssl_tls.c to use the callback variant when parsing the server's certificate chain during the handshake

The third patch is the critical one: the server sends its full certificate chain during the TLS handshake, and Mbed TLS parses it internally using mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_der(). There is no public API to inject a callback at this point. The only way to make it work is to modify ssl_tls.c directly — a library-internal file that is not meant to be patched by consumers.

This is not a theoretical concern. It is the exact reason issue #7182 was filed and why PR #7846 has been stalled since 2023: threading a callback through the TLS handshake layer is architecturally invasive and difficult to land.

What RFC 5280 says

Section 4.2.1.4 is explicit on this point:

To promote interoperability, this profile RECOMMENDS that policy information terms consist of only an OID. Where an OID alone is insufficient, this profile strongly recommends that the use of qualifiers be limited to those identified in this section.

And regarding enforcement:

The certificate policies extension [...] indicates the policy under which the certificate has been issued and the purposes for which the certificate may be used. [...] Applications with specific policy requirements are expected to have a list of those policies which they will accept and to compare the policy OIDs in the certificate to that list.

In other words: the certificate declares its policies, and the application decides whether to accept them. The parser's job is to parse, not to enforce.

What every other major TLS library does

OpenSSL, GnuTLS, wolfSSL, and BoringSSL all parse certificates with arbitrary policy OIDs without error. They store the OIDs and leave enforcement to the application. Mbed TLS is the only widely-used TLS library that rejects certificates at parse time for having an unrecognized policy OID.

This creates a real interoperability gap. Certificates that work with every other TLS stack fail with Mbed TLS — not because they are malformed or insecure, but because the parser is enforcing a policy decision that belongs to the application layer.

Why the callback approach is not sufficient

The extension callback mechanism (mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_der_with_ext_cb) was designed as a general-purpose escape hatch, and it works well for that purpose. However, it is not a viable solution for certificate policies during TLS handshakes because:

- The handshake parser is internal. ssl_tls.c calls mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_der() directly. There is no way to pass a callback without modifying the library.
- Every consumer must independently discover and implement the workaround. Each project using Mbed TLS with GSMA, enterprise PKI, or any non-anyPolicy certificates must figure out the same three-patch solution.
- The workaround is fragile. It requires coordinated patches across the TLS library and the platform layer (e.g., Zephyr), and those patches must be maintained across library upgrades.
- It inverts the principle of least surprise. Developers reasonably expect that a valid, standards-compliant certificate will parse successfully. Having to register a callback just to accept a certificate that every other TLS library handles transparently is unexpected and underdocumented.

What the proposed change does

The fix is minimal and self-contained:

1. Remove the anyPolicy check in x509_get_certificate_policies() — parse and store all policy OIDs, return success
2. Simplify the CERTIFICATE_POLICIES case in x509_get_crt_ext() — remove the callback fallback and critical/non-critical error handling
3. Display unknown OIDs as dotted decimal in x509_info_cert_policies() — e.g., 2.23.146.1.2.1.0 instead of ???

The net result is 12 fewer lines of code, zero new API surface, and full backward compatibility for any application that was already successfully parsing certificates. Applications that need to enforce specific policies can still inspect crt->certificate_policies after parsing — exactly as RFC 5280 intends.

  Summary

  ```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┐
│               Aspect                │        Callback workaround        │ Accept all policy OIDs │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Lines changed                       │ ~50 across 3 files in 2 codebases │ ~45 in 1 file + tests  │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Requires patching ssl_tls.c         │ Yes                               │ No                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Requires patching platform code     │ Yes (e.g., Zephyr sockets_tls.c)  │ No                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Works during TLS handshake          │ Only with library modification    │ Out of the box         │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ RFC 5280 compliant                  │ Yes (via workaround)              │ Yes (by design)        │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Consistent with other TLS libraries │ No (unique to Mbed TLS)           │ Yes                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Maintenance burden on consumers     │ Per-project, per-upgrade          │ None                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘
```

OpenSSL

- File: crypto/x509/v3_cpols.c — r2i_certpol() accepts any valid OID via OBJ_txt2obj(), stores it in a POLICYINFO structure. No filtering.
- Policy checking is disabled by default (X509_V_FLAG_POLICY_CHECK must be explicitly set).
- Notably, Issue #21359 documented that OpenSSL accepts even malformed critical policy data. The Technical Oversight Committee decided this was by design — applications are assumed to have made this policy choice by accepting the permissive defaults.

GnuTLS

- File: lib/x509/x509_ext.c — gnutls_x509_ext_import_policies() iterates all policy identifiers and stores them unconditionally. Unknown OIDs are processed identically to recognized ones.
- The GNUTLS_CERT_UNKNOWN_CRIT_EXTENSIONS flag only applies to extensions GnuTLS doesn't recognize at all — since it recognizes the Certificate Policies extension (OID 2.5.29.32), it parses it successfully even when critical, regardless of which policy OIDs are inside.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
wolfSSL

- File: wolfcrypt/src/asn.c — DecodeCertPolicy() parses and stores all policy OIDs (up to CTC_MAX_CERTPOL_NB = 2).
- Requires WOLFSSL_CERT_EXT to be enabled. When enabled, Certificate Policies is a "known" extension, so it's parsed successfully even when critical.
- Without WOLFSSL_CERT_EXT, it logs "Certificate Policy extension not supported yet" and would return ASN_CRIT_EXT_E if critical.

BoringSSL

- File: crypto/x509/v3_cpols.cc — Same approach as OpenSSL (it's a fork). r2i_certpol() accepts any OID. No rejection of unknown policy OIDs.
- David Benjamin (Google/BoringSSL) authored RFC 9618 updating the policy validation algorithm — even that RFC does not recommend rejecting unknown OIDs at parse time.

  Summary table

```
┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┐
│         Library         │ Rejects unknown policy OIDs? │ Policy enforcement at parse time? │ Default policy checking? │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ OpenSSL                 │ No                           │ No                                │ Off                      │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ GnuTLS                  │ No                           │ No                                │ Off                      │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ wolfSSL                 │ No                           │ No                                │ Off                      │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ BoringSSL               │ No                           │ No                                │ Off                      │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ Mbed TLS (before patch) │ Yes                          │ Yes                               │ Yes                      │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ Mbed TLS (after patch)  │ No                           │ No                                │ Off                      │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┘
```

The callback mechanism remains available for truly unknown or custom extensions. Certificate policies are not that — they are a well-defined, standard extension with clear RFC guidance that enforcement belongs to the application, not the parser.

Resolves https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/10534

Add unused fields to some structs so that we can use them if needed within the lifetime of the 4.1 LTS. Add a union of a `size_t` and a `void *`, allowing us to use either type to extend the structure.

The structs with added fields are:
* `mbedtls_ssl_session`
* `mbedtls_ssl_config`
* `mbedtls_ssl_context`
* `mbedtls_x509_crt`

The ABI checker has failed, but that's exactly the point.

## Description

Validate wildcard hostname against certificate CN. Perform domain suffix matching for certificates. This allows a configured CN (eg., "[any].example.com") to match any subdomain like "sub.example.com", "api.example.com", etc. The function extracts the domain portion from the provided hostname (everything after the first dot) and compares it case-insensitively against the CN suffix present in the certificate.

items.title: mbedtls: Mention compiler optimization in the threat model

items.body:
## Description

Fixes #10668

Fix a TLS 1.2 client regression introduced by the signature-algorithm validation added in commit 9f19fe1874d9e48ac56c7b4a65bdd95c0dee236c.

The new validation correctly rejects signature algorithms that are invalid for TLS 1.2 or were not offered by the client, but it also rejects valid `rsa_pss_rsae_*` SignatureScheme values even when RSA-PSS support is compiled in. As a result, handshakes with TLS 1.2 servers using RSA-PSS signatures can fail with `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER` (`-0x6600`).

This PR updates the TLS 1.2 support check to accept `rsa_pss_rsae_sha256/384/512` when the corresponding RSA-PSS and hash support is available, and adds regression tests for these values.

## PR checklist

- [x] **development PR** provided here
